coredata.dc:title: Multi-wavelength emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent nanosensing platform for the detection of ascorbic acid and their application in bioimaging tracking
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Lin, Kun-Peng
Zhang, Yi
Zhu, Pei-De

coredata.dc:description: Vitamins are indispensable active components of the human body, and their abnormal levels are often closely related to various diseases. Therefore, searching for ultra-sensitive and accurate vitamin detection strategies is essential and significant for clinical examination and diagnosis. In this paper, multi-wavelength emission fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using four-leaf green fluorescent extract, O-phenylenediamine, and β-cyclodextrin as the raw materials, and N, N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. The CDs can achieve controllable fluorescence changes with multi-wavelength emission at different excitation wavelengths and exhibit good photoluminescence optical properties. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak intensities of the N-doped CDs at 400, and 470 nm excitation wavelengths decreased with increased AA content. They showed good linearity in the 0.6–1.4, and 0.1–0.5 μM ranges, with detection limit coefficients of 0.12, and 0.14 μM, respectively. Moreover, due to their excellent photostability and good biocompatibility, N-doped CDs have been successfully applied to the bioimaging and biosensing of exogenous AA in 4T1 cells, as well as in vivo imaging tracking of Kunming (KM) mice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Detection
Ascorbic acid
Imaging

Analysis of AA in environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characteristics of N-doped CDs. (a) HRTEM, (b) particle size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, (d) AFM, (e) particle size height distribution, and (f) 3D images of N-doped CDs.

Structural analysis of N-doped CDs. (a) Fourier transform infrared spectra, (b) XPS total spectra, (c) high-resolution C 1s XPS, (d) N 1s XPS, (e) O 1s XPS spectra, and (f) fluorescence emission spectra of N-doped CDs.
The fluorescence emission spectra of AA and CDs at 400, and 470 nm excitation wavelengths and linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and AA content.
Confocal imaging of exogenous AA in 4T1 cells. In vitro fluorescence imaging of 4T1 cells incubated with N-doped CDs (a) and N-doped CDs (b) in AA medium in vitro. Scale bar: 20 μm.
Schematic synthesis of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs and their use as nanofluorescence sensing platforms for the detection of AA.
Fluorescence lifetime parameters of N-doped CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Multi-wavelength emission N doped CDs synthesized in this study were used as fluorescent probes to analyze the variation of AA content in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Multi-wavelength emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent nanosensing platform for the detection of ascorbic acid and their application in bioimaging tracking

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Henan University, Kaifeng, Henan 475004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Henan University, Kaifeng, Henan 475004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clinical Medical Center of Tissue Engineering and Regeneration, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang, Henan 453003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Clinical Medical Center of Tissue Engineering and Regeneration

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Clinical Medical Center of Tissue Engineering and Regeneration, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang, Henan 453003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Senior Department of Orthopedics, The Fourth Medical Center of PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100142, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Fourth Medical Center of PLA General Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Senior Department of Orthopedics, The Fourth Medical Center of PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100142, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science and Engineering, Xinyang College, Xinyang, Henan 464000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science and Engineering, Xinyang College, Xinyang, Henan 464000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have successfully synthesized multi-wavelength emitting fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in the ranges of 0.6–1.4 and 0.1–0.5 μM, with detection limit coefficients of about 0.12 and 0.14 μM, respectively, and have demonstrated good recoveries and reliability of AA detection in actual water samples.

Vitamins are indispensable active components of the human body, and their abnormal levels are often closely related to various diseases. Therefore, searching for ultra-sensitive and accurate vitamin detection strategies is essential and significant for clinical examination and diagnosis. In this paper, multi-wavelength emission fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using four-leaf green fluorescent extract, O-phenylenediamine, and β-cyclodextrin as the raw materials, and N, N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. The CDs can achieve controllable fluorescence changes with multi-wavelength emission at different excitation wavelengths and exhibit good photoluminescence optical properties. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak intensities of the N-doped CDs at 400, and 470 nm excitation wavelengths decreased with increased AA content. They showed good linearity in the 0.6–1.4, and 0.1–0.5 μM ranges, with detection limit coefficients of 0.12, and 0.14 μM, respectively. Moreover, due to their excellent photostability and good biocompatibility, N-doped CDs have been successfully applied to the bioimaging and biosensing of exogenous AA in 4T1 cells, as well as in vivo imaging tracking of Kunming (KM) mice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is found in many biological systems, foods, and multivitamin supplements and is one of the most critical substances necessary for maintaining human physiological health [1–3]. As an essential micronutrient and antioxidant, AA scavenges free radicals and has been implicated in crucial processes of human metabolism, including immune function, cell differentiation, collagen formation, tyrosine, cartilage regeneration, and tryptophan metabolism. In addition, AA plays an essential role in cellular physiological activities and homeostasis [4,5]. For example, AA has a protective effect against free radical-induced diseases and cellular damage, and its abnormal levels usually lead to the development of cardiovascular, Parkinson's, muscle weakness, joint swelling, and edema [6,7]. However, since the human body lacks the most crucial enzyme for vitamin biosynthesis, L-guloyltannic acid-1, 4-endolipid oxidase, and cannot produce AA independently but can only obtain it from fresh fruits and vegetables, the AA content of food has become an essential indicator of food freshness and nutritional quality [7,8]. Therefore, there is a need to seek a fast-track routine method to quantify changes in AA content, which is crucial for accurate guidance of a healthy human diet.

There are many related types of research for identifying AA in food, live cells, and other samples, such as electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorescence, and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods [4,7,9,10]. Among these methods, fluorescence methods have received more and more attention and favor from researchers due to their unique advantages [4,11–13]. Therefore, in recent years, many sensors based on nanoluminescent materials have been widely developed for sensitivity analysis of metal ions and biomolecules and show the advantages of simple operation and high sensitivity, including semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), fluorescent metal nanoclusters (MNCs), and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [11,12]. However, heavy metal ion doping and nanoparticle size significantly affect the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the aforementioned fluorescent nanomaterials, limiting their practical application in AA detection [11]. In contrast, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), due to their unique optical properties, surface chemistry, biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, have been used in a wide range of applications in the fields of drug delivery, catalysis, sensing, nanomaterials fabrication, and cancer therapy, especially in the areas of identification and analyte detection, overcoming the shortcomings of traditional metal-based fluorescent nanosensors [11]. For example, Li et al. [14]. using poly (vinyl pyridinium alkenone), citric acid, and methionine as precursor materials, successfully prepared fluorescent CDs with high selectivity and ultra-sensitivity as a fluorescent nanosensor for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions and AA by a hydrothermal method; Chen et al. [15]. developed novel fluorescent CDs that can rapidly, reversibly, and dynamically detect hypochlorite and AA using fluorescence and colorimetric methods, which could exhibit good linearity for hypochlorite in the range of 0.1–100 μM with a limit of detection coefficient of 83 nM and was used for the selective recovery of hypochlorite via AA; Yang et al. [16] prepared fluorescent CDs with surfaces enriched with amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups and utilized the unique properties of the surface of the CDs for specific and ultra-sensitive analysis of changes in the content of nitrite and AA. However, most of these methods rely on single-wavelength or ratio fluorescence sensing, the accuracy of which is susceptible to external environmental and internal factors [17,18]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop multi-wavelength emission fluorescent CDs, observe the fluorescence intensity ratio of the emission peak by changing the excitation wavelength of the fluorescent CDs, and perform ultrasensitive and rapid detection and analysis of triggering factors to improve the detection sensitivity and reliability, thus realizing the advantages of complementarity.

As mentioned earlier, most conventional fluorescence methods use single-wavelength or proportional fluorescence sensors to detect changes in AA content. This paper synthesized multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs by a one-step solvothermal method using O-phenylenediamine, β-cyclodextrin, and four-leaf green as precursors. Based on the unique photoluminescence property of CDs, a multi-wavelength emission fluorescence nanosensing platform for AA detection was constructed (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescent nanosensing platform showed good linear relationships with AA in the ranges of 0.6–1.4 and 0.1–0.5 μM with detection limit coefficients of 0.12 and 0.14 μM at the excitation wavelengths of 400 and 470 nm, respectively. In addition, we explored the sensitivity and specificity of the multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs for AA and found that the probe has good anti-interference. Finally, we used multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs to detect and analyze changes in exogenous AA content in 4T1 cells and for in vivo fluorescence imaging tracking in a breast cancer tumor model. Overall, in this paper, multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs were used as fluorescent nanoprobes for the first time for the analysis and imaging of AA, providing a preliminary basis for preparing and applying multi-wavelength emission fluorescent CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs were synthesized from O-phenylenediamine, β-cyclodextrin, and fluorescein extracts by a simple and environmentally friendly method. Firstly, we used relevant instruments to characterize the synthetic materials' morphology and observed the materials' particle size and morphological height. As displayed in the HRTEM and particle size distribution plots in Fig. 1 a and 1b, the multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs exhibited good mono-dispersity and particle with a size of approximately 2.2 nm (Fig. 1b). In addition, as shown in Fig. 1c, the synthesized material shows a strong diffraction peak at 17.5°, which may be related to the elemental valence bond structure on the surface of the N-doped CDs, indicating that they are composed of amorphous carbon [19,20]. Moreover, we further analyzed the N-doped CDs’ particle heights and 3D images using AFM. As shown in Fig. 1d, e, and f, the particle height of the synthesized material is about 3.5 nm, which indicates that the particle height of the multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs is somewhat different from the measured particle sizes in Fig. 1a and b, suggesting that the CDs have a spherical-like structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Photostability is a prerequisite for fluorescent materials as nanosensing platforms. Therefore, it is crucial to discuss in detail the optical properties of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs under different conditions. As displayed in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs at various excitation wavelengths shows a trend of increasing and then decreasing with the increase of NaCl concentration. Therefore, we chose 0.04 mol/L NaCl solution as the optimal condition for analyzing the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs at different times and pH. As shown in Fig. S7, under UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs showed almost no significant change in 60 min, indicating that the material has excellent photostability and can be used in biomedical fields. However, the fluorescence intensity of the N-doped CDs gradually weakened as the pH increased from 2 to 12 (Fig. S8). Thus, pH 5 was chosen as the optimal condition for the in vivo and in vitro detection of AA.

Under optimal conditions, we recorded the changes in fluorescence emission spectra of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs with increasing AA content at 350, 400 and 470 nm excitation wavelengths. As shown in Figs. S9, the fluorescence intensity of the N-doped CDs at 474 nm (Ex = 350 nm) varied chaotically with increasing AA content, with either enhanced or diminished variations, and had no potential as a fluorescent probe for the detection of AA. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peaks at 495 nm (Ex = 400 nm) (Fig. 3 a) and 580 nm (Ex = 470 nm) (Fig. 3b) showed a decreasing trend with increasing AA content. Moreover, compared with the changes in the emission peak intensities of blue/yellow fluorescent CDs, the changes in the emission peak intensities of red fluorescent CDs containing nitrogen- and oxygen-related functional groups in defective states were relatively noticeable, which could be attributed to the interaction the between nitrogen-related functional groups on the surface of the CDs and AA, which brought them close to each other and resulted in the transfer of the energy of the emission state of the red fluorescent CDs to the AA. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, and 3d, the fluorescence intensity ratios (ΔF/F0) of the N-doped CDs showed an excellent linear relationship with the AA content in the ranges of 0.6–1.4 and 0.1–0.5 μM at the excitation wavelengths of 400, and 470 nm. The linear correlation equations were y = 0.0258x + 0.1239, and y = 0.0392x + 0.6084, with the linear correlation coefficients of 0.9829 and 0.9642, respectively (ΔF = Fx-F0, with F0 and Fx being the N-doped CDs in the absence and presence of AA at 400 and 470 nm excitation wavelengths). The detection limits were 0.12, and 0.14 μM (S/N = 3), much lower than those reported in the relevant literature. In conclusion, compared with other fluorescent probes, the multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs could be used for the fluorescence sensing analysis of AA in the range of 0.6–1.4 and 0.1–0.5 μM, with the detection limits of 0.12 and 0.14 μM, respectively, which showed the multi-responsive and reliable detection properties (Table S2).

We selected different metal ions and biomolecules for interference correlation experiments to evaluate the anti-interference performance of the probe for the detection of AA. As shown in Figs. S10 and S11, the fluorescence intensity change ratios of N-doped CDs for other metal ions and biomolecules, such as GSH, Lys, Cys, and Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, In3+, and Zn2+, were all less than 6.5 %. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity change ratios of N-doped CDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions were somewhat higher, at −7.8 % (Ex = 400 nm) and −5.5 % (Ex = 470 nm), respectively, but still much smaller than the fluorescence intensity change ratios of N-doped CDs for the detection of AA, at −12.8 % (Ex = 400 nm) and −9.5 % (Ex = 470 nm), respectively, indicating that N-doped CDs at the excitation wavelengths of 400 and 470 nm were the most suitable for the detection of AA. This good AA selectivity may be related to the internal filtering effect between the two. Therefore, the method can be used to monitor and analyze AA in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AA in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the variation of AA content in real water samples to analyze the potential application of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs as fluorescent probes for AA detection in real samples. First, we briefly processed real water samples and selected three AA concentration points at 400, and 470 nm excitation wavelengths in each linear range, respectively. Subsequently, different contents of AA were sequentially added to the solution containing N-doped CDs separately to explore the fluorescence intensity changes of N-doped CDs at 400 and 470 nm excitation wavelengths. Finally, the changes in AA content in the real water samples were calculated with the help of the linear relationship functions in Fig. 3c and 3d. The recoveries of AA in real water samples were between 94.36 and 107.2 %, as shown in Table 2 , and the standard deviations were between 1.8 and 5.4 %, which indicated that the sensor has good reliability for the analysis of AA in real water samples.

To explore the prospect of synthetic materials as fluorescent probes in biomedicine, we evaluated the toxicity of N-doped CDs on 4T1 cells in vitro by the MTT method. As shown in Fig. S12, the survival rate of 4T1 cells was significantly decreased after the addition of N-doped CDs at different concentrations (0–1500 μg/mL), and the half-maximal inhibitory concentration was about 420 μg/mL, indicating that N-doped CDs had relatively good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [21]. Therefore, we performed in vitro fluorescence imaging experiments on 4T1 cells to further verify the practicability of N-doped CDs as fluorescent probes in vivo and in vitro. As shown in Fig. S13, after incubation 200 μg/mL N-doped CDs with 4T1 cells at 37 °C for different times (2, 4, 8, and 12 h), it was found that the N-doped CDs exhibited four kinds of fluorescence, namely, purple, green, yellow, and red, and had the most vigorous fluorescence intensity at 8 h, which indicated that the N-doped CDs had the optical properties of multi-wavelength emission. Therefore, we further analyzed using N-doped CDs as fluorescent probes for exogenous AA detection. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs in 4T1 cells became weaker with the increase of AA content, indicating that N-doped CDs can be successfully used to detect and analyze exogenous AA in 4T1 cells, which is promising for application. In addition, the in vivo fluorescence imaging results of Kunming (KM) mice are shown in Fig. S14, where the N-doped CDs showed promising in vivo fluorescence imaging in a mouse tumor model, suggesting that the probe has the potential to be applied as a contrast agent for real-time imaging of in vivo tumor models.

Using a simple and green solvothermal method, we used small molecules as precursor materials and N, N-dimethylformamide as a solvent to synthesize novel multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs. By changing the excitation wavelength, the multi-wavelength emission fluorescent CDs achieved controllable emission changes from purple to red fluorescence, exhibiting good photoluminescence performance. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak intensities of the N-doped CDs at 495 (Ex = 400 nm) and 580 nm (Ex = 470 nm) gradually decreased with the increase of AA content. They showed good linear relationship in the ranges of 0.6–1.4 and 0.1–0.5 μM, with the corresponding detection limit coefficients of 0.12 and 0.14 μM, respectively, which exhibited good sensitivity and anti-interference performances. Due to the excellent physicochemical properties of multi-wavelength emission fluorescent N-doped CDs, this probe can be used for detecting and analyzing exogenous AA in 4T1 cells and in vivo bioimaging tracking of KM mice, which is expected to provide a new method for the detection and analysis of AA and provide a powerful analytical tool for the study of intracellular ROS-related diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFB4706305), National Defense Science and Technology Excellence Youth Science Fund Program (2022-JCJQ-ZQ-018), Doctoral Start-up Funds for Xinxiang Medical University (505554), and Xinyang College Research Funding Project (2022-XJLYB-007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-template molecularly surface imprinted polymer on fluorescent metal-organic frameworks functionalized with carbon dots for ascorbic acid and uric acid detection

Ali, O.B.A. Shatery, Dual-template molecularly surface imprinted polymer on fluorescent metal-organic frameworks functionalized with carbon dots for ascorbic acid and uric acid detection, Spectrochim. Acta A 291 (2023) 122340, Doi: 10.1016/j.saa.2023.122340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-mode detection of ascorbic acid in orange juice by N/S doped carbon dots based on cellulose acetate

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploration of pH-responsive carbon dots for detecting nitrite and ascorbic acid

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hg2+-mediated ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots for imaging glutathione in living cells and zebrafish

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Chang, Z.H. Zhao, W.Y. Li, H. Shi, Y.X. Yang, L.H. Shi, S.N. Shuang, Hg2+-mediated ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots for imaging glutathione in living cells and zebrafish, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 10(30) (2022) 10068-10076, Doi: 10.1021/acssuschemeng.2c03173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive dual fluorophore-conjugated carbon dots for intracellular pH sensing in 3D tumor models

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.K. Tan, H. Chan, Z.F. Lu, H. Zreiqat, G. Lakhwani, P. Lesani, E.J. New, Ultrasensitive dual fluorophore-conjugated carbon dots for intracellular pH sensing in 3D tumor models, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 16 (2024) 477303-47313, Doi: 10.1021/acsami.4c10836.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step green approach to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from banana juice for selective detection of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Chaudhary, P.K. Gupta, S. Eremin, P.P. Solanki, One-step green approach to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from banana juice for selective detection of copper ions, J. Environ. Chem. Eng. 8 (2020) 103720, Doi: 10.1016/j.jece.2020.103720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface charge-dependent cytokine production using near-infrared emitting silicon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chinnathambi, N. Shirahata, P. Lesani, V. Thangavel, G.N. Pandian, Surface charge-dependent cytokine production using near-infrared emitting silicon quantum dots, Sci. Rep. 14 (2024) 9618, Doi: 10.1038/s41598-024-60536-2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot with co-doped nitrogen and phosphorus for tazettine ratiometric fluorescent sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J Fang, Y.S. Wu, J. Yang, N. Wang, K.T. Mo, C.J. Zeng, B.Y. Deng, Carbon quantum dot with co-doped nitrogen and phosphorus for tazettine ratiometric fluorescent sensing, Spectrochim. Acta A 306 (2024), 123616, Doi: 10.1016/j.saa.2023.123616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots with peroxidase-mimetic activity for ascorbic acid detection through both fluorometric and colorimetric methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, J.J. Zeng, D. Guo, L. Liu, L.W. Xiong, X.G. Luo, Z.Y. Hu, F.S. Wu, Cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots with peroxidase-mimetic activity for ascorbic acid detection through both fluorometric and colorimetric methods, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 13 (2021) 49453-49461, Doi: 10.1021/acsami.1c13198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift are viable fluorescent probes for detection and cellular imaging of silver ions and glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.Y. Yan, Z.J. Bai, F.L. Zu, Y. Zhang, X.D. Sun, T.C. Ma, L. Chen, Yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift are viable fluorescent probes for detection and cellular imaging of silver ions and glutathione, Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 113, Doi: 10.1007/s00604-018-3221-8.
